data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "A rapid, region-wide hyperarid climate shift around 1200 BC caused severe agricultural failures in the Eastern Mediterranean, while Egypt's reliance on the Nile flood insulated it.", "mechanism": "Sharp decrease in precipitation leading to drought, crop failure, population stress, and eventual societal collapse.", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit", "Levantine city‑states"], "excludes": ["Egypt"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Paleo‑hydrological data from speleothems in Anatolia showing no significant drought at 1200 BC", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "Climate records from the Mediterranean are mixed; a clear, simultaneous drought would strongly support this model, but many high‑resolution proxies suggest only modest variability."}
